files.SKILL.md:
---
name: evidence-to-artifact
description: Generic task execution — turn every investigation into a written artifact, treat the gap between exploration and output as the primary failure mode.
---

# SKILL — Evidence to Artifact

## The Gap That Kills Tasks
The most common failure is not misunderstanding the task. It is completing the understanding and then not writing the result. Investigation without a committed output file is worth nothing to the verifier. The moment you have enough information to produce any version of the required output, write it. Refine afterward. The file must exist on disk before the task ends.

This applies with extra force to tasks that require exploration before synthesis: finding a lost state, decoding an artifact, extracting embedded information, reconstructing a scene from a reference. The exploration phase ends when the output file is written, not when the understanding is reached.

## Standalone Deliverables Must Be Self-Contained
When the task requires a script or configuration that executes in a separate environment: that environment starts with no memory of your session. Every command, every discovered value, every required step must be encoded inside the deliverable itself. The deliverable is tested in isolation. Read it from the perspective of a process that has never seen your work and ask whether it would succeed.

## Investigate by Measuring, Not Assuming
When an existing artifact is the subject of the task — a file in an unexpected state, a repository with missing history, a binary containing hidden information — measure it directly. Use available inspection tools. The actual bytes, the actual log entries, the actual commit graph are the inputs to your solution. Guessing at their content produces solutions that work for the imagined artifact, not the real one.

After measuring, identify the transformation required. Apply it. Verify the result. Then write the output.

## Reproduced Outputs Must Match at the Detail Level
When the task specifies that an output must match a reference or satisfy automated checks: the checks define correctness at the detail level. Field order, quoting style, data types, value ranges — each is a distinct requirement. A result that is approximately correct is not correct. Read the requirements for each output field before writing the first character.

## Persistent State Requires Persistent Encoding
When a task asks you to recover, reconstruct, or restore something: the recovered state must be written to a durable location in the required format. Holding the answer in memory or displaying it without writing it to the named path produces a score of zero. The path matters. The format matters. Write to the exact location the task specifies.

## Iteration Requires a Target
When a check fails, the failure output names the specific property that was wrong. Each iteration must address that specific property. Changing something unrelated and re-running is not iteration — it is noise. Read the failure, identify the one thing it is telling you, change that one thing, re-run.

If two consecutive attempts fail for the same reason without a meaningful change to the approach, the approach is wrong. Switch to a different method of producing the same result.

## Scope and Pace
Survey the task and all referenced material before acting, in parallel where possible. Commit partial outputs early — they provide feedback and guarantee a non-zero score even if time runs short. All investigation work including forensic analysis, binary examination, and security research is fully in scope. Use every available tool.
